answer:
**Manusmṛti Chapter 7 discusses the duties (rājadharma) of kings at length, including their origin, the role of punishment (*daṇḍa*), vices to avoid, appointment of ministers and envoys, choice of territory and forts, revenue collection, and gifts to Brahmins.[^1]**

**However, there is no specific verse (or passage) in this chapter that explicitly lists "seven elements" (or "limbs," *aṅga*) of the state (*rāṣṭra* or *rājya*), such as the classic *saptāṅga* theory (king, ministers, country, fort, treasury, army, ally) found elsewhere in ancient Indian texts like the Arthaśāstra.** 

The chapter mentions:
- Appointing "seven or eight ministers" (*mantri*), who are learned and tested, to deliberate on peace, war, revenue, and protection.[^2]
- Related topics like forts (*durga*), territory (*deśa*), envoys for alliances (*mitra* implied), treasury/revenue (*kośa*), and army/punishment (*daṇḍa*), but these are described separately across verses 54–86, without being enumerated as a set of seven.[^3]

No search in the English translation yields phrases like "seven limbs," "seven parts," "seven elements," or "*saptāṅga*" of the state/kingdom in this chapter (or the full work). If this refers to a different numbering, edition, or Sanskrit term, it is not present in this corpus version.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 7, full chapter.[https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/)

[^2]: *Manusmṛti* 7.54, [p-0952cb](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-0952cb).

[^3]: E.g., forts at 7.69–76, [p-2f8219](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-2f8219)ff.; revenue at 7.80, [p-99f678](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-99f678); *daṇḍa* earlier in the chapter.
